column1: # Source: Z-Beam Laser Cleaning Research Lab
# Citation: Z-Beam (2025). PET Laser Cleaning Dataset. Retrieved from http://localhost:3000/materials/plastic/thermoplastic/pet-laser-cleaning

column9: Low power for thermal-sensitive polymer cleaning
Larger spot for gentle surface treatment
Lower frequency to prevent thermal damage
Low energy density to avoid melting
Short pulses for minimal thermal impact
Fast scanning to reduce heat accumulation
Multiple gentle passes for gradual cleaning

Typical range for polymer cleaning to avoid thermal damage; starting at 100W for light contamination on PET, max below ~300W to prevent melting (PET melting point ~260°C). Source: Studies on Nd:YAG laser cleaning of plastics (e.g., J. Laser Appl. 2018).

Effective speeds balance cleaning efficiency and heat input; slower (500-1000 mm/s) for heavy contamination, faster (2000+ mm/s) for light; 1500 mm/s optimal for uniform PET surface prep. Source: Industry manuals from IPG Photonics and Laserax equipment specs.

Nanosecond regime preferred for 1064 nm cleaning of PET to minimize HAZ; picosecond (ps) or femtosecond (fs) for ultra-precise but costlier; 20 ns balances ablation and low thermal diffusion (PET low conductivity ~0.15 W/mK). Source: Laser physics reviews (Opt. Lasers Eng. 2020).

Typical for pulsed cleaning; 20-50 kHz recommended for PET to enable fast processing without excessive heat accumulation; higher rates increase speed but risk substrate heating. Source: Academic literature on polymer laser ablation (Appl. Surf. Sci. 2019).

Fluence below PET damage threshold (~2-4 J/cm² at 1064 nm) for safe contaminant removal; 0.5-1.5 J/cm² for light cleaning, up to 2.5 for heavy; conservative to account for PET's moderate IR absorption (~20-30%). Source: Ablation threshold studies (J. Phys. D: Appl. Phys. 2017).

Smaller spots (100-300 μm) for fine detail on PET, larger (500-1000 μm) for broad areas; 300 μm recommended for balanced resolution and energy distribution in industrial cleaning. Source: Manufacturer specs from Coherent and Trumpf laser systems.

Single pass for light contamination, multi-pass (2-3) for complete removal without damage; 2 passes typical for PET to ensure uniformity while minimizing heat exposure. Source: Case studies in industrial laser cleaning (Proc. SPIE 2021).
Ensures uniform coverage; 40-60% for PET to avoid gaps or overexposure; higher (70-80%) for heavy contamination, lower for speed. Source: Process optimization in laser scanning (Opt. Eng. 2016).
